romania.title: Ucrainean găsit în stare critică, după ce a fost abandonat într-un pârâu de grupul cu care a trecut granița prin Munţii Maramureşului
romania.summary: Un tânăr ucrainean care a trecut graniţa în România, prin Munţii Maramureşului, împreună cu un grup de cetăţeni din ţara vecină, a fost abandonat de către compatrioţii săi, într-un pârâu, în momentul în care, epuizat fiind, nu s-a mai putut deplasa. Ciobani de la o stână au solicitat ajutorul autorităţilor, salvamontiştii maramureşeni găsindu-l pe bărbat în stare critică. Acesta a primit primul ajutor de la salvatorii montani, fiind apoi evacuat de pe munte cu elicopterul.

romania.summary: McDonald’s® a dat startul celei de-a doua ediții Grant My Passion, programul care susține tinerii să își transforme pasiunile în realitate. Prin granturi, workshopuri și mentorat, tinerii sunt sprijiniți să… Articolul Prin Grant My Passion, ediția 2026, McDonald’s acordă granturi în valoare totală de peste 350.000 de lei (brut), destinate susținerii inițiativelor din cinci domenii-cheie: antreprenoriat, educație, talent, sustenabilitate și creare de conținut apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

În brașov, de exemplu, din ce în ce mai mulți nepalezi se plâng că urșii le percep o taxă de protecție, constând în 50% din comenzile transportate. „La început se mulțumeau doar cu câte […] The post Nepalezii din Brașov, nevoiți să le dea urșilor jumătate din mâncarea livrată ca taxă de protecție appeared first on Times New Roman .

Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... medical.title: Potential gene therapy for late-stage Parkinson's side-effects uncovered
medical.summary: Northwestern Medicine scientists have discovered that suppressing excitatory synaptic transmission in a small group of neurons in the brain may reverse levodopa-induced dyskinesia in patients with late-stage Parkinson's disease without reducing the symptomatic benefits of levodopa treatment, according to a recent study published in Neuron.

medical.title: Infertility tied to early menopause onset
medical.summary: The average woman spends more than one-third of her life in menopause. Some women, however, must deal with menopause-related symptoms longer and face added health problems because of premature menopause (before age 40) or early menopause (before age 45). A new study found that infertility may be a risk factor for early menopause. Results of the study are published online today in Menopause.

medical.title: Life-changing benefits of hydroxyurea for sickle cell anemia affirmed by 10-year study
medical.summary: Fewer serious complications. Fewer hospitalizations and blood transfusions. Better growth and development. And a markedly lower risk of death from the complications of sickle cell anemia.

medical.title: Air pollution may be harming your brain's 'encyclopedia'
medical.summary: A new study by researchers at UC Davis Health and Kaiser Permanente found that higher exposure to very small air pollution particles (PM2.5) over a 17-year span was associated with lower semantic memory. Semantic memory acts like the brain's "encyclopedia" for things like facts, words and long-term general knowledge.

medical.title: Living in rural areas associated with worse health for people with epilepsy
medical.summary: For people with epilepsy, living in counties that are more rural was associated with poorer health outcomes than for people living in more urban counties, according to a study published in Neurology. People living in the most rural counties hospitalized for epilepsy had nearly twice the odds of dying in the hospital than those living in the most urban counties. The study does not prove that living in rural areas causes worse health; it only shows an association.

medical.title: AI tool simplifies and scales complete genome assembly, supporting advances in diagnostics and precision medicine
medical.summary: An international research team led by the A*STAR Genome Institute of Singapore (A*STAR GIS) has developed HERRO, an artificial intelligence (AI) tool that could make it easier and more cost-effective to produce complete, high-quality genome assemblies.

medical.title: The road to better health requires a map of the microbes in your mouth
medical.summary: There's a lot of buzz about the gut microbiome—the trillions of microbes that help us digest food and support the immune system. But your mouth is also home to its own highly specialized microbial community, and new research suggests that these oral microbes may play an equally important role in maintaining health. The work is published in the journal Microbiology Spectrum.

medical.title: Ingestive behaviors: How people sip water and eat may be tracked by wearable sensors on the wrist and jaw
medical.summary: University of Rhode Island graduate student Adeleke Justin Akinkurolere will present ground-breaking research on ingestive behaviors at NUTRITION 2026, an international gathering of nutrition professionals on July 25–28 in National Harbor, Maryland.

medical.title: To reduce anxiety during pregnancy, make sleep a priority
medical.summary: Postpartum and perinatal depression are known challenges for those going through pregnancy, but there has been less focus on the more prevalent disorder of anxiety.

medical.title: Study shows how the fetal environment may reduce the risk of type 1 diabetes
medical.summary: Researchers at Umeå University have shown in a new study that the environment in the womb can leave lasting imprints on the immune system and influence the risk of developing Type 1 diabetes, even when the genetic background is the same. The findings refine our understanding of how genetic and early environmental factors interact in autoimmune diseases.

medical.title: Americans agree that ultraprocessed foods pose a major health hazard
medical.summary: Across partisan lines, Americans broadly believe ultraprocessed foods are addictive and harmful—expressing attitudes strikingly like those that spurred regulation of the tobacco industry, new Cornell-led research finds.

medical.title: To curb overprescribing for seniors, researchers urge annual prescription checkups
medical.summary: To address the growing problem of overprescribing for seniors, a new Canadian guideline is calling for routine medication reviews. In Canada, roughly two in three adults age 65 or older take five or more medications. While often necessary, complex drug regimens can increase the risk of side effects and affect quality of life.

medical.title: How big tobacco helped shape the design of ultra-processed foods
medical.summary: A new UC San Francisco study reveals how Philip Morris Companies Inc. used cigarette research, flavor engineering, and behavioral science to turn Lunchables into one of America's most successful ultra-processed foods for children.

medical.title: Study finds being female is not a universal stroke risk factor for patients with AFib
medical.summary: A new Tulane University study challenges a long-standing assumption in heart care: that being female automatically increases stroke risk for patients with atrial fibrillation, a common condition that causes the heart to beat irregularly.

medical.title: Novel vaccine adjuvant could make it easier to eradicate polio
medical.summary: In the United States, children routinely receive an injectable form of the polio vaccine. This vaccine is very effective at preventing illness, but it doesn't block transmission of the polio virus as well as the oral polio vaccine does.

science.title: Integrating citizen science with experimental data uncovers how switchgrass adapts flowering by region
science.summary: In its native habitat, switchgrass flowered earlier when growing farther north. In experiments with diverse genetic samples, it flowered earlier in the south.

science.title: Sunrise III data release opens rare high-altitude solar views that could sharpen space weather forecasts
science.summary: Close to 100 scientists from all over Europe, the United States, and Japan are gathering this week at the Institute for Solar Physics (KIS) in Freiburg to present and discuss the first scientific results from the successful third flight of the balloon-borne solar observatory SUNRISE. KIS is a key contributor to the SUNRISE project. The meeting marks a milestone in the study of the sun by bringing together researchers from all over the world who are analyzing unprecedented new data gathered by the SUNRISE III project.

science.title: Dogs respond to human tone without words, hinting at communication older than language
science.summary: Humans can communicate various instructions to dogs without using actual words—simply by modulating the tone of their voice, a new study from ELTE University's Department of Ethology shows. By repeating the nonsense syllable 'bü' in different intonations, humans successfully signaled "Yes," "No," "Here," and "There" and, remarkably, dogs responded correctly, despite receiving no prior training. The findings reveal ancient acoustic codes, interpretable across species, that predate language itself. The study was published in Cognition.

science.title: Arctic river deltas face rising climate pressure while holding vast frozen carbon reserves
science.summary: Many rivers flow into the Arctic Ocean north of the Arctic Circle—including the Lena in Siberia and the Mackenzie River in Canada. The deltas of these large and small rivers store large amounts of carbon, which is bound there in frozen soils and sediments. Climate change, however, is destabilizing the deltas from the ocean and land side and also from the air.

science.title: Giant fan-shaped structure found under East Antarctica
science.summary: An international team of researchers including our Department of Geography has discovered a vast geological structure hidden beneath the East Antarctic Ice Sheet. The findings are published in the journal Nature Geoscience.

science.title: Real-time fish interaction enlarges young guppy brains, while screen time falls short
science.summary: Young guppies who were able to see and interact with live fish developed larger brains than guppies who only saw other fish on a screen. This is shown in a new study from Stockholm University, published in Biology Letters. The findings suggest that live social interaction in real time may be important for brain development.

science.title: Cleaner recycling method unlocks reusable plastics from mixed packaging
science.summary: Scientists from Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (NTU Singapore) have developed a new method to recycle mixed plastic packaging without using harmful chemical solvents—an approach that could make one of the world's most difficult waste streams significantly easier to handle.

science.title: Aluminum oxide's irregular atomic surface explains its low reactivity
science.summary: Why do certain surfaces behave very differently from what theoretical calculations suggest? Scientists long assumed that the aluminum oxide surface should be highly reactive and capable of splitting water molecules. In experiments, however, this behavior is barely observed.

science.title: Out-of-plane ice bridges reveal new way to suppress frost spreading
science.summary: A research team led by Professor Nenad Miljkovic in The Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign has published a breakthrough study in Nature Physics. The work reports the first experimental discovery of a previously unknown frost propagation mechanism—a "suspended ice bridge"—offering new pathways for anti-frosting surface design.

science.title: 8 out of 10 northern fulmar seabirds have plastic in their stomachs, finds study
science.summary: Plastic pollution is widespread in the world's oceans. A new study of northern fulmars from the North Atlantic shows that plastic pollution is also common in northern marine areas. The research is published in the journal Marine Pollution Bulletin.

science.title: 'Don't scare the cat!' Engineers find smarter way to measure quantum systems
science.summary: UNSW Sydney engineers have riffed on the famous Schrödinger's cat analogy to demonstrate a more efficient way to eliminate errors in quantum computing.

science.title: Armed with AI, study identifies prey from predator crunching sounds
science.summary: Interactions between hard-shelled marine mollusks such as clams and snails and their predators play a critical but largely unseen role in shaping coastal ecosystems. These organisms help stabilize shorelines, filter water and support biodiversity, making them foundational to coastal health. Yet they are increasingly threatened by ocean acidification and expanding populations of mobile shell-crushing predators.

science.title: NASA Finds New Way Earth May Have Received Elements Needed for Life
science.summary: NASA-supported scientists have provided new information about how the early Earth may have acquired some elements necessary for the planet to become habitable. They also suggest a new role for Jupiter in the distribution of these elements throughout the young solar system. The study, published today in Science Advances, examines this history by looking at […]

science.title: SWOT satellite gets clearer ocean data after fix for hidden underwater wave interference
science.summary: Florida State University research published in Science Advances demonstrates a new framework for predicting the motion of kilometer-scale underwater waves that complicate satellite readings of the ocean.

science.title: Overarming America: Game theory explores how fear and social pressure drive gun purchases
science.summary: A Dartmouth College study is the first to map the interplay of personal choice and social networks that has led to the United States being one of the world's most heavily armed countries, with 120 firearms for every 100 people. The researchers describe in Science Advances how individual incentives to buy firearms can lead to a phenomenon they call "overarming." In an overarmed society, the collective cost of firearm ownership outweighs the individual benefits of possessing a gun.

science.title: Abortion restrictions associated with lower female medical school applicant numbers
science.summary: States with restrictive abortion policies saw slower growth in the proportion of female medical school applicants following the 2022 reversal of Roe v. Wade, according to a new study published in the open-access journal PLOS Global Public Health by Amrit Kirpalani of Western University, Canada, and colleagues.

science.title: Teen well-being improving after years of post-pandemic concern, major study finds
science.summary: A major new study of more than 115,000 young people suggests teenage well-being may finally be recovering after years of concern over the long-term impact of the COVID-19 pandemic.

science.title: Egypt fossils show modern ocean fish rose rapidly after dinosaur extinction
science.summary: The extinction that ended the Age of Dinosaurs is best known for clearing the way for the Age of Mammals on land. Scientists have long suspected that the same catastrophe also transformed life in the seas, opening ecological space for the rise of modern marine fish faunas. Yet the timing and geography of that transition have remained uncertain because of the sparse fossil record.

science.title: Traditional, patriarchal Japanese terms for husband and wife may now be perceived as neutral
science.summary: A new study suggests that, for modern Japanese speakers, two traditional, patriarchal words for "husband" ("shujin," literally meaning "master") and "wife" ("kanai," "inside-the-house") may be losing their original meanings, though men in the study evaluated both traditional and neutral words for "husband" more positively than words for "wife."

science.title: Printed manga may give the brain a storytelling advantage
science.summary: A new study by researchers at the University of Tokyo explores whether reading manga on paper or on a tablet changes how the brain understands and remembers stories. Participants first read the opening half of a two-part manga story either on paper or on a tablet. Later, while inside an MRI scanner, they read the second half through LCD goggles and answered questions about the story.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: Dacă e joi, e CINEVARA! 9 cinefili. 9 filme. 9 feluri de a evada din realitate.
mix_items.summary: Din 4 iunie până pe 30 iulie 2026, în fiecare joi, CINEVARA #8 te bagă mereu în alt film! Programul gratuit de cinema de cartier în căști, coordonat de Rezidența9,… Articolul Dacă e joi, e CINEVARA! 9 cinefili. 9 filme. 9 feluri de a evada din realitate. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Jazz in the Park anunță programul festivalului din Cluj-Napoca. Artiști din 14 țări la cea de-a 14-a ediție a celui mai iubit festival de jazz din România
mix_items.summary: București, 3 iunie 2026 – Au mai rămas doar două zile până la începerea celui mai iubit festival de jazz din România. Jazz in the Park 2026 anunță programul celor… Articolul Jazz in the Park anunță programul festivalului din Cluj-Napoca. Artiști din 14 țări la cea de-a 14-a ediție a celui mai iubit festival de jazz din România apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: Ucrainean găsit în stare critică, după ce a fost abandonat într-un pârâu de grupul cu care a trecut granița prin Munţii Maramureşului
mix_items.summary: Un tânăr ucrainean care a trecut graniţa în România, prin Munţii Maramureşului, împreună cu un grup de cetăţeni din ţara vecină, a fost abandonat de către compatrioţii săi, într-un pârâu, în momentul în care, epuizat fiind, nu s-a mai putut deplasa. Ciobani de la o stână au solicitat ajutorul autorităţilor, salvamontiştii maramureşeni găsindu-l pe bărbat în stare critică. Acesta a primit primul ajutor de la salvatorii montani, fiind apoi evacuat de pe munte cu elicopterul.

mix_items.title: 😂 Şoşoacă, fraţii Tate şi Steven Seagal participă la Concursul de Frumuseţe de la Sankt Petersburg
mix_items.summary: Indiferent că e război sau pace, ce-i frumos şi lui Dumnezeu îi place! „Las Vegasul Rusiei”, Sankt Petersburg, găzduieşte în această perioadă Concursul Internaţional de Frumuseţe Ivan Cel Groaznic 2026, la care sunt aşteptaţi să participe unii dintre cei mai sexy oameni în viaţă. Printre personalităţile care şi-au anunţat prezenţa la „Davosul frumuseţii” se numără […] The post Şoşoacă, fraţii Tate şi Steven Seagal participă la Concursul de Frumuseţe de la Sankt Petersburg appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Prin Grant My Passion, ediția 2026, McDonald’s acordă granturi în valoare totală de peste 350.000 de lei (brut), destinate susținerii inițiativelor din cinci domenii-cheie: antreprenoriat, educație, talent, sustenabilitate și creare de conținut
mix_items.summary: McDonald’s® a dat startul celei de-a doua ediții Grant My Passion, programul care susține tinerii să își transforme pasiunile în realitate. Prin granturi, workshopuri și mentorat, tinerii sunt sprijiniți să… Articolul Prin Grant My Passion, ediția 2026, McDonald’s acordă granturi în valoare totală de peste 350.000 de lei (brut), destinate susținerii inițiativelor din cinci domenii-cheie: antreprenoriat, educație, talent, sustenabilitate și creare de conținut apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

mix_items.title: 😂 Nepalezii din Brașov, nevoiți să le dea urșilor jumătate din mâncarea livrată ca taxă de protecție
mix_items.summary: Înmulțirea urșilor și vizitele lor din ce în ce mai dese în zone populate produc schimbări importante inclusiv pe piața muncii. În brașov, de exemplu, din ce în ce mai mulți nepalezi se plâng că urșii le percep o taxă de protecție, constând în 50% din comenzile transportate. „La început se mulțumeau doar cu câte […] The post Nepalezii din Brașov, nevoiți să le dea urșilor jumătate din mâncarea livrată ca taxă de protecție appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Asistent Social
mix_items.summary: SOS Serviciul de Asistență Comunitară Constanţa Locație: MEDGIDIA, jud. Constanţa Număr de poziții: 2 Contract: full time Grija și încrederea unei familii, emoție pozitivă, zâmbete, suport și încurajare, nevoi de bază îndeplinite și vise transformate... The post Asistent Social appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: 😂 Socului Kebap, contract de 174 de milioane € din SAFE pentru hrănirea generalilor români
mix_items.summary: Veste excelentă venită dinspre Ministerul Apărării: o sumă importantă din pachetul SAFE pentru înzestrarea armatei române va ajunge la o firmă din România! Este vorba de Socului Kebap, care s-a ales cu un contract de 174 de milioane de euro, bani pentru care va trebui să asigure hrănirea generalilor români. Generalii Armatei Române au optat […] The post Socului Kebap, contract de 174 de milioane € din SAFE pentru hrănirea generalilor români appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026
mix_items.summary: Zbor Hub Brașov a găzduit finala națională Arena Urșilor Junior 2026, o competiție dedicată liceenilor cu idei de afaceri curajoase și planuri antreprenoriale inovatoare. Brașovul a devenit, pentru câteva ore, … Read More The post Brașovul a devenit capitala tinerilor antreprenori din România la Arena Urșilor Junior 2026 appeared first on Stiri pozitive .

mix_items.summary: București, 20 mai 2026: Fundația World Vision România, împreună cu Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași, a inaugurat o seră... Articolul World Vision România investește în formarea viitorilor specialiști ai agriculturii românești. O seră didactică modernizată cu tehnologii de Agricultură 4.0, inaugurată la Liceul Agricol „Sandu Aldea” din Călărași. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor
mix_items.summary: Atelierele în comunitate organizate de Fundația Inocenți îi ajută pe copii să își dezvolte încrederea în sine, abilitățile emoționale și relațiile sociale. The post Ateliere în comunitate, sprijin pentru dezvoltarea copiilor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.summary: În România, mulți copii încă se tem să vorbească despre abuzurile prin care trec. Conform unui studiu al Fundației World... Articolul Doar 1 din 2 copii ar anunța poliția în cazul unui abuz. World Vision România: Copiii care raportează trebuie sprijiniți și încurajați, nu umiliți public. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România
mix_items.summary: Fundația World Vision România solicită Camerei Deputaților respingerea proiectul de lege privind finanțarea ONG-urilor, adoptat de Senat (L233/2026). Inițiativa legislativă... Articolul World Vision România: Propunerea legislativă privind finanțarea ONG-urilor aduce măsuri disproportionate care riscă să afecteze copiii vulnerabili din România apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Integrating citizen science with experimental data uncovers how switchgrass adapts flowering by region
mix_items.summary: In its native habitat, switchgrass flowered earlier when growing farther north. In experiments with diverse genetic samples, it flowered earlier in the south.

mix_items.title: Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi
mix_items.summary: Ionuț, elev în clasa a XI-a la un liceu din Municipiul Calafat, pasionat de muzică, cu un glas folcloric aparte,... Articolul Gândirea civică – concertul unei generații: povestea lui Ionuț, un tânăr în fața provocărilor cu care se confruntă tinerii de astăzi apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Potential gene therapy for late-stage Parkinson's side-effects uncovered
mix_items.summary: Northwestern Medicine scientists have discovered that suppressing excitatory synaptic transmission in a small group of neurons in the brain may reverse levodopa-induced dyskinesia in patients with late-stage Parkinson's disease without reducing the symptomatic benefits of levodopa treatment, according to a recent study published in Neuron.

mix_items.title: Psihopedagog/Pedagog
mix_items.summary: Locație: Bacău Fii adultul care poate schimba viitorul unui copil Dacă ai răbdare, empatie și îți dorești un job cu adevărat valoros, în care fiecare zi contează pentru dezvoltarea unor... The post Psihopedagog/Pedagog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Infertility tied to early menopause onset
mix_items.summary: The average woman spends more than one-third of her life in menopause. Some women, however, must deal with menopause-related symptoms longer and face added health problems because of premature menopause (before age 40) or early menopause (before age 45). A new study found that infertility may be a risk factor for early menopause. Results of the study are published online today in Menopause.

mix_items.title: Agent telemarketing (team leader)
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Sunrise III data release opens rare high-altitude solar views that could sharpen space weather forecasts
mix_items.summary: Close to 100 scientists from all over Europe, the United States, and Japan are gathering this week at the Institute for Solar Physics (KIS) in Freiburg to present and discuss the first scientific results from the successful third flight of the balloon-borne solar observatory SUNRISE. KIS is a key contributor to the SUNRISE project. The meeting marks a milestone in the study of the sun by bringing together researchers from all over the world who are analyzing unprecedented new data gathered by the SUNRISE III project.

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Dogs respond to human tone without words, hinting at communication older than language
mix_items.summary: Humans can communicate various instructions to dogs without using actual words—simply by modulating the tone of their voice, a new study from ELTE University's Department of Ethology shows. By repeating the nonsense syllable 'bü' in different intonations, humans successfully signaled "Yes," "No," "Here," and "There" and, remarkably, dogs responded correctly, despite receiving no prior training. The findings reveal ancient acoustic codes, interpretable across species, that predate language itself. The study was published in Cognition.

mix_items.title: Life-changing benefits of hydroxyurea for sickle cell anemia affirmed by 10-year study
mix_items.summary: Fewer serious complications. Fewer hospitalizations and blood transfusions. Better growth and development. And a markedly lower risk of death from the complications of sickle cell anemia.

mix_items.title: Air pollution may be harming your brain's 'encyclopedia'
mix_items.summary: A new study by researchers at UC Davis Health and Kaiser Permanente found that higher exposure to very small air pollution particles (PM2.5) over a 17-year span was associated with lower semantic memory. Semantic memory acts like the brain's "encyclopedia" for things like facts, words and long-term general knowledge.

mix_items.title: Arctic river deltas face rising climate pressure while holding vast frozen carbon reserves
mix_items.summary: Many rivers flow into the Arctic Ocean north of the Arctic Circle—including the Lena in Siberia and the Mackenzie River in Canada. The deltas of these large and small rivers store large amounts of carbon, which is bound there in frozen soils and sediments. Climate change, however, is destabilizing the deltas from the ocean and land side and also from the air.

mix_items.title: Living in rural areas associated with worse health for people with epilepsy
mix_items.summary: For people with epilepsy, living in counties that are more rural was associated with poorer health outcomes than for people living in more urban counties, according to a study published in Neurology. People living in the most rural counties hospitalized for epilepsy had nearly twice the odds of dying in the hospital than those living in the most urban counties. The study does not prove that living in rural areas causes worse health; it only shows an association.

mix_items.title: Giant fan-shaped structure found under East Antarctica
mix_items.summary: An international team of researchers including our Department of Geography has discovered a vast geological structure hidden beneath the East Antarctic Ice Sheet. The findings are published in the journal Nature Geoscience.

mix_items.title: Real-time fish interaction enlarges young guppy brains, while screen time falls short
mix_items.summary: Young guppies who were able to see and interact with live fish developed larger brains than guppies who only saw other fish on a screen. This is shown in a new study from Stockholm University, published in Biology Letters. The findings suggest that live social interaction in real time may be important for brain development.

mix_items.title: AI tool simplifies and scales complete genome assembly, supporting advances in diagnostics and precision medicine
mix_items.summary: An international research team led by the A*STAR Genome Institute of Singapore (A*STAR GIS) has developed HERRO, an artificial intelligence (AI) tool that could make it easier and more cost-effective to produce complete, high-quality genome assemblies.

mix_items.title: The road to better health requires a map of the microbes in your mouth
mix_items.summary: There's a lot of buzz about the gut microbiome—the trillions of microbes that help us digest food and support the immune system. But your mouth is also home to its own highly specialized microbial community, and new research suggests that these oral microbes may play an equally important role in maintaining health. The work is published in the journal Microbiology Spectrum.

mix_items.title: Ingestive behaviors: How people sip water and eat may be tracked by wearable sensors on the wrist and jaw
mix_items.summary: University of Rhode Island graduate student Adeleke Justin Akinkurolere will present ground-breaking research on ingestive behaviors at NUTRITION 2026, an international gathering of nutrition professionals on July 25–28 in National Harbor, Maryland.

mix_items.title: Cleaner recycling method unlocks reusable plastics from mixed packaging
mix_items.summary: Scientists from Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (NTU Singapore) have developed a new method to recycle mixed plastic packaging without using harmful chemical solvents—an approach that could make one of the world's most difficult waste streams significantly easier to handle.

mix_items.title: Aluminum oxide's irregular atomic surface explains its low reactivity
mix_items.summary: Why do certain surfaces behave very differently from what theoretical calculations suggest? Scientists long assumed that the aluminum oxide surface should be highly reactive and capable of splitting water molecules. In experiments, however, this behavior is barely observed.

mix_items.title: To reduce anxiety during pregnancy, make sleep a priority
mix_items.summary: Postpartum and perinatal depression are known challenges for those going through pregnancy, but there has been less focus on the more prevalent disorder of anxiety.

mix_items.title: Out-of-plane ice bridges reveal new way to suppress frost spreading
mix_items.summary: A research team led by Professor Nenad Miljkovic in The Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign has published a breakthrough study in Nature Physics. The work reports the first experimental discovery of a previously unknown frost propagation mechanism—a "suspended ice bridge"—offering new pathways for anti-frosting surface design.

mix_items.title: 8 out of 10 northern fulmar seabirds have plastic in their stomachs, finds study
mix_items.summary: Plastic pollution is widespread in the world's oceans. A new study of northern fulmars from the North Atlantic shows that plastic pollution is also common in northern marine areas. The research is published in the journal Marine Pollution Bulletin.

mix_items.title: Study shows how the fetal environment may reduce the risk of type 1 diabetes
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Umeå University have shown in a new study that the environment in the womb can leave lasting imprints on the immune system and influence the risk of developing Type 1 diabetes, even when the genetic background is the same. The findings refine our understanding of how genetic and early environmental factors interact in autoimmune diseases.

mix_items.title: 'Don't scare the cat!' Engineers find smarter way to measure quantum systems
mix_items.summary: UNSW Sydney engineers have riffed on the famous Schrödinger's cat analogy to demonstrate a more efficient way to eliminate errors in quantum computing.

mix_items.title: Americans agree that ultraprocessed foods pose a major health hazard
mix_items.summary: Across partisan lines, Americans broadly believe ultraprocessed foods are addictive and harmful—expressing attitudes strikingly like those that spurred regulation of the tobacco industry, new Cornell-led research finds.

mix_items.title: To curb overprescribing for seniors, researchers urge annual prescription checkups
mix_items.summary: To address the growing problem of overprescribing for seniors, a new Canadian guideline is calling for routine medication reviews. In Canada, roughly two in three adults age 65 or older take five or more medications. While often necessary, complex drug regimens can increase the risk of side effects and affect quality of life.

mix_items.title: Armed with AI, study identifies prey from predator crunching sounds
mix_items.summary: Interactions between hard-shelled marine mollusks such as clams and snails and their predators play a critical but largely unseen role in shaping coastal ecosystems. These organisms help stabilize shorelines, filter water and support biodiversity, making them foundational to coastal health. Yet they are increasingly threatened by ocean acidification and expanding populations of mobile shell-crushing predators.

mix_items.title: How big tobacco helped shape the design of ultra-processed foods
mix_items.summary: A new UC San Francisco study reveals how Philip Morris Companies Inc. used cigarette research, flavor engineering, and behavioral science to turn Lunchables into one of America's most successful ultra-processed foods for children.

mix_items.title: NASA Finds New Way Earth May Have Received Elements Needed for Life
mix_items.summary: NASA-supported scientists have provided new information about how the early Earth may have acquired some elements necessary for the planet to become habitable. They also suggest a new role for Jupiter in the distribution of these elements throughout the young solar system. The study, published today in Science Advances, examines this history by looking at […]

mix_items.title: Study finds being female is not a universal stroke risk factor for patients with AFib
mix_items.summary: A new Tulane University study challenges a long-standing assumption in heart care: that being female automatically increases stroke risk for patients with atrial fibrillation, a common condition that causes the heart to beat irregularly.

mix_items.title: SWOT satellite gets clearer ocean data after fix for hidden underwater wave interference
mix_items.summary: Florida State University research published in Science Advances demonstrates a new framework for predicting the motion of kilometer-scale underwater waves that complicate satellite readings of the ocean.

mix_items.title: Overarming America: Game theory explores how fear and social pressure drive gun purchases
mix_items.summary: A Dartmouth College study is the first to map the interplay of personal choice and social networks that has led to the United States being one of the world's most heavily armed countries, with 120 firearms for every 100 people. The researchers describe in Science Advances how individual incentives to buy firearms can lead to a phenomenon they call "overarming." In an overarmed society, the collective cost of firearm ownership outweighs the individual benefits of possessing a gun.

mix_items.title: Abortion restrictions associated with lower female medical school applicant numbers
mix_items.summary: States with restrictive abortion policies saw slower growth in the proportion of female medical school applicants following the 2022 reversal of Roe v. Wade, according to a new study published in the open-access journal PLOS Global Public Health by Amrit Kirpalani of Western University, Canada, and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Teen well-being improving after years of post-pandemic concern, major study finds
mix_items.summary: A major new study of more than 115,000 young people suggests teenage well-being may finally be recovering after years of concern over the long-term impact of the COVID-19 pandemic.

mix_items.title: Egypt fossils show modern ocean fish rose rapidly after dinosaur extinction
mix_items.summary: The extinction that ended the Age of Dinosaurs is best known for clearing the way for the Age of Mammals on land. Scientists have long suspected that the same catastrophe also transformed life in the seas, opening ecological space for the rise of modern marine fish faunas. Yet the timing and geography of that transition have remained uncertain because of the sparse fossil record.

mix_items.title: Traditional, patriarchal Japanese terms for husband and wife may now be perceived as neutral
mix_items.summary: A new study suggests that, for modern Japanese speakers, two traditional, patriarchal words for "husband" ("shujin," literally meaning "master") and "wife" ("kanai," "inside-the-house") may be losing their original meanings, though men in the study evaluated both traditional and neutral words for "husband" more positively than words for "wife."

mix_items.title: Novel vaccine adjuvant could make it easier to eradicate polio
mix_items.summary: In the United States, children routinely receive an injectable form of the polio vaccine. This vaccine is very effective at preventing illness, but it doesn't block transmission of the polio virus as well as the oral polio vaccine does.

mix_items.title: Printed manga may give the brain a storytelling advantage
mix_items.summary: A new study by researchers at the University of Tokyo explores whether reading manga on paper or on a tablet changes how the brain understands and remembers stories. Participants first read the opening half of a two-part manga story either on paper or on a tablet. Later, while inside an MRI scanner, they read the second half through LCD goggles and answered questions about the story.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Ce mai faci? - Imi urmaresc obiectivele. De obicei ma depasesc.

satire_ro.title: DNA doarme în papuci! E primul Bookfest la care nu se lansează nicio carte scrisă în puşcărie
